insight: Smorcus (likely Scorchilla or similar red unit) is best built before the opponent has a Blastforge, not after. It functions primarily as a threat and a way to tech out of red rather than as a direct damage dealer, though at 5RR for 1 attack it is still cost-efficient thanks to the implied breach threat.
author: leary93
context: Discussion about how to respond to DDE openings and how aggressive red units function.

insight: Fastimus (fast Animus opening) typically runs around 11 drones with sequences like DD/DA/DTT or DDT, and does not ramp to many drones because the game ends too quickly for heavy economic investment. A slow path to 13 drones with Blastforge for Walls is an alternative but less common line.
author: crash_overlord
context: Player asking how Fastimus works in terms of drone count and tech sequencing.

insight: In base set, the accepted winning line as Player 2 is Fastimus (DD/DA). Slow Animus as P2 (e.g. DD/DD/DDA) is considered very inefficient. Natural Animus and Lucina are noted exceptions to the Fastimus dominance.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion of whether Immolite or Tarsier is better and what the baseline efficient opening looks like.

insight: Immolite is generally weaker than Tarsier in the long run due to lower sustained damage output, but is equal or better when the game ends in 3 or fewer turns, or when an immediate attack is critical (e.g. forcing the opponent to hold a Drone or sacrifice a 2-stamina Rhino). Immolite can also be correct when only 6 gold is available and you would otherwise waste a red.
author: consensus
context: Player asking why Immolite is considered better than Tarsier after buying Immolites led to slow attack ramp.

insight: Odin is at its best when it is NOT being clicked, because it functions as a large absorber. The exception is Xaetron, where you can alternate which unit absorbs each turn, making clicking Odin more viable. In a base+Odin+Matrix set, shadourow argues it can be correct to click Odin every turn if you are already above absorb without it, effectively trading a Steelsplitter for a Wall+Engineer, with Wall supply being important.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about when it is correct to click Odin versus hold it as an absorber, especially in sets with Matrix.

insight: Over-defending Odin is only slightly worse than sacrificing it in front of a better absorber. In a Defense Grid set where Odin is the second-best absorber, it is worth keeping around after the grid dies so it can absorb afterwards. Keeping Odin also allows throwing Steelsplitters in the turns immediately before starting to breach.
author: mrguy888
context: Follow-up discussion on Odin usage in sets with Defense Grid and other large absorbers.

insight: In a set with Xaetron and Matrix, amalloy notes it is likely better to absorb on Matrix every turn and click Xaetron rather than alternating absorbers between the two. Clicking Xaetron generates 5 Gauss charges (roughly 2.75 damage/turn) while absorbing on it only gives back 4 extra absorb every other turn, making the click better if your normal absorb already exceeds roughly 3.
author: amalloy
context: Mathematical analysis of Xaetron vs Matrix usage in sets containing both units.

insight: Apollo sniping Perforators is extremely poor value: it costs 13BBB with BT2 to deal 2 damage per turn, which is worse than sniping Tarsiers (already a losing play). Apollo should snipe Tarsiers only if it nets or saves from a breach, and sniping an Animus can be good against red-consuming units or Iceblades but is still weaker than targeting a Wall.
author: namington
context: Thread analyzing whether buying cheap sub-3HP units can beat Apollo and what Apollo should target.

insight: Cynestra should almost never be purchased in a set where Apollo is still a viable option for the opponent. If the opponent is forced to commit away from Apollo (e.g. via early pressure), then Cynestra becomes acceptable. The core issue is that Cynestra gets sniped by Apollo, negating most of its value, though a single Cynestra that gets sniped in ~4 turns may still have generated enough damage to be worthwhile.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of what offensive units are viable in sets where Apollo is present.

insight: Apollo is not viable in base-set-only games due to the lack of soak support. In sets with Apollo, strong soak units like Plexo Cell or Polywall are needed for the Apollo player to sustain the strategy; without such soak, cheap sub-3HP attackers can beat Apollo.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion of defensive options needed to make Apollo viable and how to beat it with cheap attackers.

insight: Doomed Drone supply is a meaningful constraint. With 2 Energy you almost always want a Doomed Drone, and with 4 Energy you want 2 Doomed Drones, but with 3 Energy a regular Drone is generally preferred except at mid-high or high inflation. Running out of Doomed Drone supply mid-game is a real cost to consider when clicking Ebb.
author: consensus
context: amalloy asking when it is correct to click Ebb to buy Doomed Drones versus keeping a regular Drone.

insight: Militia is generally better than Steelsplitter when you can get any absorb denial from it (2-3 Militias are better than Splitters), but Steelsplitter is better with Odin or Manticore in the set, and arguably better against heavy red decks. Militia also provides better breach resistance while Splitters are better when you need to switch to defense quickly or want synchronized attacks.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion comparing Militia and Steelsplitter as 5-gold attackers.

insight: Arka Sodara functions like a large Omega Splitter rather than a normal Steelsplitter: it is generally better held for absorb than clicked, because clicking it costs 7 defense tempo immediately while the opponent only loses 4 defense tempo half a turn later. Clicking Arka is most justified when you can kill a vigilant unit without needing to buy extra defense, or when you are significantly overdefended.
author: consensus
context: Extended discussion of when to click Arka Sodara versus holding it as an absorber.

insight: Urban Sentry and Xeno Guardian are acceptable soak targets when you are floating blue mana or anticipate running out of Walls, but dealing damage with them is strictly better than soaking. Soaking on Borehole Patroller is particularly undesirable due to its low health. Urban Sentry is more resilient against multiple buys and is especially strong against chill threats.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion of whether to soak damage on Urban Sentry, Xeno Guardian, and Borehole Patroller versus Walls.

insight: Tia Threnody, once fully built, effectively always wins the game but getting to Tia can be too costly if the opponent is applying heavy early pressure with units like Doomed Mech and Blood Pact. In sets with those units, a mono-blue strategy with possibly one Conduit for Forcefields is often stronger than attempting the Animus into Tia route.
author: crash_overlord
context: Set analysis with Vai Mauronax, Tia Threnody, Doomed Mech, and Blood Pact present.
